coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric nanoprobe based on silver nanoclusters and carbon dots for the fluorescent detection of biothiols
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shuming
Lin, Bixia
Yu, Ying
Cao, Yujuan
Guo, Manli
Shui, Lingling
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescent probes could eliminate the influence from experimental factors and improve the detection accuracy. In this article, a ratiometric nanoprobe was constructed based on silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and used for the detection of biothiols. The fluorescence peak of AgNCs was observed at 650nm with excitation wavelength at 370nm. In order to construct the ratiometric fluorescent probe, NCDs with the excitation and emission wavelengths at 370nm and 450nm were selected. After adding AgNCs, the fluorescence of NCDs was quenched. The mechanism of the fluorescence quenching was studied by fluorescence, UV–Vis absorption and the fluorescence lifetime spectra. The results indicated that the quenching could be ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE). With the addition of biothiols, the fluorescence of AgNCs at 650nm decreased due to the breakdown of AgNCs, and the fluorescence of NCDs at 450nm recovered accordingly. Thus, the relationship between the ratio of the fluorescence intensities (I450/I650) and biothiol concentration was used to establish the determination method for biothiols. Cysteine (Cys) was taken as the model of biothiols, and the working curve for Cys was I450/I650 =0.60CCys −1.86 (CCys: μmol/L) with the detection limit of 0.14μmol/L (S/N=3). Then, the method was used for the detection of Cys in human urine and serum samples with satisfactory accuracy and recovery ratios. Furthermore, the probe could be applied for the visual semi-quantitative determination of Cys by naked eyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric nanoprobe
Combinatorial probe
Fluorescence analysis
Visual semi-quantitation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Cys in Actual Samples
Construction of the Ratiometric Nanoprobe and Mechanism
The Conditions Optimization of Detecting Cys
Determination of Cys in Actual Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The excitation and emission spectra of AgNCs (1, 2) and NCDs (3, 4). (b) The fluorescence spectra of the AgNCs/NCDs probe with constant concentration of 0.28mg/mL NCDs and different concentrations of AgNCs. (c) The fluorescence spectra of NCDs (1), AgNCs (2), AgNCs/NCDs (3), AgNCs/NCDs with the addition of Cys (4). (d) Time-resolved decay curves of NCDs in the absence and presence of AgNCs.
Optimization of the detection conditions. (a) The mass ratio of AgNCs and NCDs. (b) The pH value. (c) The incubation temperature. (d) The reaction time.

The working curve and selectivity of the method for detecting biothiols. (a) Fluorescence spectra (λex =370nm) of the nanoprobe on the addition of increasing concentrations of Cys. Inset: the amplified spectra from 560 to 730nm. (b) The work curves for detecting Cys and GSH. (c) Interferences 1 and (d) interferences 2. The concentration of Cys and interference was 120μmol/L except acetonitrile (48mmol/L). Blue bars: I0 and I represented the intensity ratio (I450/I650) of AgNCs/NCDs in the absence and presence of interferences, respectively. Red bars: I0 and I represented the intensity ratio (I450/I650) of AgNCs/NCDs with the addition of Cys in the absence and presence of interferences, respectively.

The photos of the ratiometric nanoprobe (a) and AgNCs (b) under the exposure to different amounts of Cys. The concentrations of Cys from left to right are 0, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300μmol/L, respectively.
The detection mechanism of biothiols based on the AgNCs/NCDs probe.
Determination of Cys in actual samples (n =3).
Comparison of the determination methods for Cys.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric nanoprobe based on silver nanoclusters and carbon dots for the fluorescent detection of biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine (Guangzhou), School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine (Guangzhou)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 378 Waihuan West Road, University City, Guangdong 510006, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescent probes could eliminate the influence from experimental factors and improve the detection accuracy. In this article, a ratiometric nanoprobe was constructed based on silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and used for the detection of biothiols. The fluorescence peak of AgNCs was observed at 650nm with excitation wavelength at 370nm. In order to construct the ratiometric fluorescent probe, NCDs with the excitation and emission wavelengths at 370nm and 450nm were selected. After adding AgNCs, the fluorescence of NCDs was quenched. The mechanism of the fluorescence quenching was studied by fluorescence, UV–Vis absorption and the fluorescence lifetime spectra. The results indicated that the quenching could be ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE). With the addition of biothiols, the fluorescence of AgNCs at 650nm decreased due to the breakdown of AgNCs, and the fluorescence of NCDs at 450nm recovered accordingly. Thus, the relationship between the ratio of the fluorescence intensities (I450/I650) and biothiol concentration was used to establish the determination method for biothiols. Cysteine (Cys) was taken as the model of biothiols, and the working curve for Cys was I450/I650 =0.60CCys −1.86 (CCys: μmol/L) with the detection limit of 0.14μmol/L (S/N=3). Then, the method was used for the detection of Cys in human urine and serum samples with satisfactory accuracy and recovery ratios. Furthermore, the probe could be applied for the visual semi-quantitative determination of Cys by naked eyes.

ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy of fluorescence analysis depend greatly on the property of fluorescent probes. Most of fluorescent probes only have a single-wavelength emission. Due to the lack of internal standard, the fluorescence signal of these probes is susceptible to the fluctuation of experimental conditions, such as stability of excitation intensity, concentration error of probes, and change of solution environment. The ratiometric fluorescent probes which have two or more emission bands at different wavelengths can overcome the above mentioned problems. The quantitative analysis by ratiometric probes is based on the intensity ratio of two emission peaks, thus the ratiometric probes have the self-calibration function. They can eliminate the influence of experimental factors and improve accuracy [1].

The traditional ratiometric probes were comprised of organic molecules with two different fluorescence peaks. The active groups of organic molecule probes could react with the analyte through coordination [2], redox, and so on [3]. The occurrence of these reactions could lead to the simultaneous changes of fluorescence intensities and wavelengths. The changes of emissions spectra were usually attributed to the mechanisms such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [4] and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [5]. Ma group had done remarkable jobs in ratiometric fluorescent probes. Therein, a ratiometric probe was designed by using propylamine and 1, 8-naphthalimide for monoamine oxidase A (MAO-A) detection. After amino of the probe reacted with MAO-A through amine oxidation and β-elimination, a new emission peak at around 550nm appeared except original 454nm. According to the relationship between the intensity ratio of these two fluorescence peaks and the concentration of MAO-A, the quantitative determination for MAO-A was carried out [6]. Besides, Ma group synthetized other ratiometric probe to detect hydrogen sulfide through reducing azido to amino [7]. Nonetheless, the construction of these ratiometric probes usually involved elaborate design, tedious synthesis, complex separation and purification [8–10].

As a new type of probes, nanoparticle fluorescent probes have recently received intense attention. Because most nanoprobes had only single emission peak, they could not serve as ratiometric probes. In recent years, a few of ratiometric nanoprobes were successfully constructed by combining two kinds of fluorescence units. For instance, Wang utilized CdTe quantum dots (QDs) of two sizes to obtain a dual-emission fluorescent hybrid sphere [11]. The red-emitting CdTe QDs were entrapped in the silica sphere as the reference unit, and the green-emitting CdTe QDs were covalently bonded to the silica surface as the response part. Tian developed a hybrid ratiometric nanoprobe by modifying the red-emitting CdTe QDs (640nm) with the blue-emitting benzothiazole fluorophore (420nm) [12]. Ouyang group used the positively charged aggregation-induced emission organic fluorescence nanoparticles (AIE-based OFNs) and negatively charged Au nanoclusters to establish a dual-emission ratiometric probe via electrostatic attraction for the detection of Hg2+ and melamine [13]. The AIE-based OFNs@AuNCs nanoprobes had the advantages of simple preparation and label-free. In this paper, a ratiometric nanoprobe was subtly constructed by combining two kinds of common nanoparticles with different emission wavelengths.

Nanosilver including silver nanoparticle and silver nanocluster was usually used to build probes for the detection of metal ions such as Cd2+ [14], Al3+ [15], Pb2+ [16], Ni2+ [17] and biothiols such as cysteine, homocysteine, glutathione [18,19]. Biothiols were small molecules with sulfydryl. They could be detected by nanosilver probes through the coordination between sulfydryl and Ag atom. Herein, in order to reduce the influence from the experimental factors, a ratiometric nanoprobe (AgNCs/NCDs) was constructed for detecting biothiols based on AgNCs and environmental-friendly NCDs. By addition of biothiols such as Cys, the decrease in fluorescence intensity of AgNCs was accompanied by a fluorescence recovery of NCDs. Then, the quantitative relationship between the intensity ratio of the two fluorescence peaks and the concentration of Cys was established. Furthermore, the probe could be used for the semi-quantitative analysis of biothiols by naked eyes. In this way, a simple and visual detection method for biothiols was established based on the AgNCs/NCDs probe.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Company. Amino acids (cysteine, leucine, phenylalanine, histidine, serine, methionine, alanine, glycine, aspartic acid, arginine, tryptophan and glutamic acid) and glutathione were obtained from Macklin Reagent Company. Citric acid, urea, acetonitrile (ACN), AgNO3, NaBH4, NaOH, Na2HPO4·12H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O were obtained from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Factory. All reagents were analytic grade unless otherwise stated. Double-distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured with a 2700 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Zeta potential was collected with Nano ZS nanometer particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed by an FLS-920 combined fluorescence lifetime and steady-state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The pictures were obtained under ultraviolet lamp (50W, Gongyi, China).

AgNCs were prepared using a modified method [20]. Briefly, 5.0mL of 0.01mol/L AgNO3 solution was gently added to 5.0mL of 50mg/mL BSA solution at room temperature under vigorous stirring. After 15min, 0.3mL of 1.0mol/L NaOH was added into the solution under continuous stirring, followed by dropwise addition of 1.0mL of fresh NaBH4 (0.01mol/L). Then the resulting solution was stirred for 2h. The obtained solution was dialyzed using 8–14kDa dialysis bag until the conductivity of the dialysate was near that of double-distilled water. The reddish brown AgNCs solution was diluted to a constant volume of 20.0mL, and the concentration of silver nanoclusters was 0.42mg/mL according to the amount of AgNO3.

NCDs were prepared according to the previous method with modification [21]. 2.1g citric acid and 1.8g urea were dissolved in 20mL of water and sealed into a 50mL teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave. After maintaining at 180°C for 4h, the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally. The black green NCDs solution was filtered through a 0.22μm microfiltration membrane to remove the black insoluble matter and dialyzed with 1kDa dialysis bag until the conductivity of the dialysate was near that of double-distilled water. Then the NCDs solution was stored at 4°C and diluted before further usage. The concentration of diluted NCDs solution was 1.65mg/mL according to the amount of citric acid.
Afterwards, the above prepared AgNCs and NCDs were mixed uniformly by the quality ratio of 1:10. Thus the AgNCs/NCDs nanoprobe was obtained and stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cys in Actual Samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human urine and serum were chosen as the actual samples to evaluate the established method. The two kinds of samples were pre-treated as follows: 10.0mL of urine sample was added with a certain amount of Cys solution, and then filtered through a 0.22μm microfiltration membrane to remove insoluble matter. 4.0mL of the filtrate was diluted 25-fold with double-distilled water. 1.0mL of serum sample was added with a certain amount of Cys solution and mixed with 1.5mL acetonitrile [22]. After vortexing for 2min and incubating for 1h, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000rpm for 15min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22μm membrane and 1.0mL of the obtained solution was diluted 50-fold.

1.2mL of AgNCs/NCDs probe, 0.5mL of Cys standard solution at different concentrations, 2.0mL of 0.01mol/L PBS at pH7.4 were added to the colorimetric tubes and diluted to a constant volume of 5.0mL with double-distilled water. After incubating at 50°C for 50min, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured by fluorescence spectrometer under slit width of 5nm, voltage of 700V and excitation wavelength of 370nm. The working curve was also plotted between the values of I450/I650 and the concentrations of Cys. The pre-treated samples were detected according to the above method. Then the spiked samples were also measured for recovery studies. The concentrations of Cys in the actual samples were calculated by the working curve. Each experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the Ratiometric Nanoprobe and Mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, a ratiometric nanoprobe was constructed by proportionally mixing two kinds of nanoparticle solutions with different fluorescence peaks. The fluorescence peak of AgNCs was observed at 650nm with excitation wavelength at 370nm (Fig. 1a). In order to construct the ratiometric fluorescent probe, another fluorescence material with the excitation wavelength at about 370nm and the emission wavelength far away from 650nm was required. On account of a broad excitation peak near 370nm and strong emission peak at 450nm (Fig. 1a), NCDs was suitable for constructing the ratiometric probe. The emission spectrum of the ratiometric nanoprobe excited at 370nm was showed in Fig. 1b. The emission peaks of 450 and 650nm could be observed clearly, which indicated that it was feasible for combining AgNCs with NCDs as a ratiometric nanoprobe.

The luminescence mechanism of the AgNCs/NCDs probe was investigated. The fluorescence intensity at 650nm was increased in the concentrations of AgNCs from 0 to 0.17mg/mL, and slightly reduced over 0.17mg/mL because of the fluorescence self-quenching (Fig. 1b). Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity of AgNCs was not affected by the addition of NCDs (Fig. 1c). However, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was quenched gradually by increasing the concentration of AgNCs from 0 to 0.28mg/mL (Fig. 1b). The interaction between two substances is usually characterized by the zeta potentials [23–25]. The zeta potentials of NCDs and AgNCs (Fig. S1) were −4.66mV and −22.40mV, respectively, so the interaction between AgNCs and NCDs was not static electricity. There was the overlap between the emission spectrum of NCDs and the UV–Vis spectrum of AgNCs which had a wide absorption peak covering 450nm (Fig. 1a). It suggested that the quenching mechanism of AgNCs on NCDs might be related to FRET or IFE. The fluorescence lifetime remained almost the same after adding different concentrations of AgNCs into the NCDs solutions (Fig. 1d and Table S1), so IFE was considered to be the quenching mechanism of the AgNCs/NCDs probe.

The detection mechanism for biothiols using this probe was explored. Cys was selected as the analyte model of biothiols. The fluorescence intensity and UV–Vis absorption of NCDs were not effected by the addition of Cys (Fig. S2). In the presence of Cys, the formation of AgS bond resulted in the breakdown of AgNCs [26]. The decreased UV–Vis absorption and quenched fluorescence of AgNCs could be observed (Fig. S3). Since IFE was weakened, the fluorescence of NCDs was recovered (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence of NCDs at 450nm recovered and the fluorescence of AgNCs at 650nm declined under the addition of Cys. Thus the ratios of I450 and I650 enhanced accompanying with the increase of Cys concentrations. Based on this mechanism, the detection of Cys could be realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Conditions Optimization of Detecting Cys
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain a better response to Cys, the detection conditions were optimized. Because the emission peaks at 450 and 650nm of AgNCs/NCDs probe changed simultaneously in the presence of Cys, using the intensity ratio of the two emission peaks (I450/I650) could overcome the influence from the fluctuation of experimental conditions. Thus, I450/I650 was employed for optimizing the detection conditions. Firstly, the optimum mass ratios of AgNCs and NCDs were studied when the concentrations of NCDs and Cys were 0.28mg/mL and 120μmol/L, respectively (Fig. 2a). When the mass ratios of AgNCs and NCDs increased, the values of I450/I650 always reduced. Due to Cys reacting with AgNCs directly, the fluorescence variations of AgNCs (△I) were served as a major indicator to optimize the mass ratios. Thus the mass ratio of 1:10 was used in the subsequent experiments. Then, the pH value was optimized from 4.0 to 9.0 in Fig. 2b. The results displayed that the value of I450/I650 reached maximum at pH7.0 and 8.0. Because the pH of biological system was mainly 7.4, 7.4 was chosen as the optimal pH. The optimization of incubation temperature was shown in Fig. 2c, I450/I650 increased gradually from 30 to 50°C and then declined from 50 to 80°C. Thus, 50°C was used as the incubation temperature. Moreover, it could be seen from Fig. 2d that the optimum reaction time was 50min.

The working curve for biothiols was plotted under the optimal detection conditions. In Fig. 3a, with increasing the concentrations of Cys, all the fluorescence curves passed the isobestic point at which the fluorescence intensity was a constant value. However, the fluorescence intensities gradually increased at 650nm and decreased at 450nm, which finally caused the enhance of I450/I650 (Fig. 3a). The value of I450/I650 was proportional to the concentration of Cys in the range of 20 to 80μmol/L. The equation was I450/I650 =0.60CCys −1.86 (CCys: μmol/L) with the correlation coefficient of 0.9975, and the detection limit was 0.14μmol/L at signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 3b). This probe was also applied to detect other biothiols such as GSH. In the concentration range of 20–80μmol/L, the linear equation for GSH was I450/I650 =0.17CGSH +5.01 (CGSH: μmol/L) with detection limit of 0.40μmol/L and correlation coefficient of 0.9923. These results showed the probe had better sensitivity for Cys than GSH.

The selectivity of the established method was evaluated. The interference of amino acids and other coexisting substances on the detection were studied under the same conditions in Fig. 3c and d. When the concentrations of the coexistent amino acids, ions and other substances was the same as that of Cys (120μmol/L) except acetonitrile (48mmol/L), the value of I450/I650 remained nearly unchanged. It indicated that the current method had certain anti-interference ability for biothiols detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cys in Actual Samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the application of the established method, AgNCs/NCDs nanoprobe was used to determine Cys in human urine and serum samples. The results measured by this method were consistent with those by Ellman method [27] in Table 1 . The recovery ratios of the spiked sample ranged between 95.4% and 106% with relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 2.2%. It revealed this method had good accuracy and repeatability for the detection of biothiols.

The AgNCs/NCDs probe was employed for the visual determination of biothiols. Under the UV lamp, the colors of the probe solutions transformed from pink to blue with the increasing concentration of Cys (Fig. 4 a). The color caused by 100μmol/L Cys was chosen as the referenced color, and other color could be compared with the referenced color to estimate the concentration of Cys. For example, when blue fluorescence was observed by naked eyes, it implied that the concentration of Cys was more than 100μmol/L. On the contrary, when red fluorescence was observed, the Cys concentration was below 100μmol/L. However, when AgNCs probe was used, the pink only turned lighter with increasing the concentration of Cys (Fig. 4b). The colors change of the ratiometric nanoprobe was more noticeable. It suggested this method could be used for the visual and semi-quantitative determination of Cys without the large equipment.

In addition, the developed method was compared with other fluorescence methods [28–30], colorimetry [31], electrochemistry [32] and surface plasmon resonance [33] for Cys determination (Table 2 ). It was noted that the construction of AgNCs/NCDs probe was simple, and the detection method had a relatively low detection limit. Furthermore, the semi-quantitative analysis of Cys could be achieved by observing the change of fluorescence color, which indicated that it could be used for visual detection of biothoils in real samples.

In this work, a ratiometric nanoprobe based on AgNCs with NCDs was obtained and applied to detect biothiols. The formation mechanism of the ratiometric nanoprobe was researched. The obtained procedure of this probe was easier than that of traditional ratiometric probes. Upon addition of biothiols, the fluorescence intensity of AgNCs declined while that of NCDs recovered. On the basis of the intensity ratio (I450/I650), a new method was established for the quantitative determination of biothiols. This detection method was simple, convenient and could be applied for biothiols in the real samples. Furthermore, it could realize the visual semi-quantitative determination of biothiols by naked eyes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575043, 21275056, 21605052, 51478196, 61574065), IRT13064, and the Platform Construction Project of Guangzhou Science Technology and Innovation Commission (No. 15180001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.01.078.
